name: GLONASS

overview.value: GLONASS is a satellite navigation system operated by Roscosmos, the Russian Federal Space Agency, tracing its origin to the Soviet Union. The system was formally declared operational in 1993 and reached its first fully operational 24-satellite constellation in 1995, though this later degraded to just 7 satellites by 2002 before recovery and modernization. The constellation flies in medium Earth orbit at approximately 19,130 km altitude across three orbital planes, each with eight satellites, inclined 64.8 degrees.

orbit.value: MEO, ~19,130 km altitude, three orbital planes of eight satellites each, 64.8 degree inclination

notes: Added 2026-07-13 on Florian's instruction, for competitive context against commercial connectivity/IoT constellations. Government-owned system: orbits deliberately set to null (MEO ~19,100 km would break the MCC 3D LEO view) and no celestrak fields filled. glonass-iac.ru (the official IAC constellation site named in the task) was fetchable and used for the website field, but its live constellation-composition table did not render through the fetch tool, so satellite-count and other facts are sourced to Wikipedia instead (unscored). service_type left null: the fetched pages give accuracy figures and per-generation signal notes but no explicit named service-tier statement comparable to GPS's SPS/PPS or Galileo's OS/HAS/PRS/SAR.

generations.name: GLONASS
generations.text: Original three-axis stabilized satellites flown 1982 to 2005, with a design lifespan of about 3 years and a mass of approximately 1,250 kg.

generations.name: GLONASS-M
generations.text: Second-generation satellites flown 2003 to 2022, with a seven-year operational lifetime, a mass of about 1,480 kg, and 52 satellites produced.

generations.name: GLONASS-K
generations.text: Satellites flown from 2011, weighing about 750 kg with a 10-year design life, transmitting modernized CDMA signals.

generations.name: GLONASS-K2
generations.text: The latest variant, in testing since 2023, with improved clock stability and signal capabilities.

events.headline: GLONASS reaches its first full 24-satellite constellation
GLONASS is declared fully operational again after modernization
